mechanism: Mechanistic claims in clinical settings are limited. Eucalyptus leaf preparations contain volatile terpenes (notably 1,8-cineole/eucalyptol) associated with secretolytic/expectorant and airway-symptom support in pharmacologic literature; EMA categorizes leaf use as traditional-use based.
safetyNotes: Per EMA monograph, avoid cineole-containing preparations in children under 2 years (laryngospasm risk). Use in pregnancy/lactation is not recommended due to insufficient safety data. Seek medical review for dyspnea, fever, or purulent sputum.

sources.title: EMA HMPC monograph: Eucalyptus globulus Labill., folium
Kew POWO: Eucalyptus globulus (accepted name and distribution)

description: Eucalyptus globulus (Tasmanian blue gum) is an Australian species used medicinally primarily via leaf preparations and essential oil constituents. Traditional use focuses on upper-respiratory symptom relief.
dosage: EMA traditional posology (adults): tea infusion 1.5-3 g leaf in 150 mL boiling water up to 4 times daily; inhalation infusion 2-3 g in 150 mL up to 3 times daily. If symptoms persist beyond 1 week, consult a clinician.
interactions: EMA monograph reports no interactions for the traditional leaf preparations described.
preparation: EMA describes comminuted leaf as herbal tea for oral use or infusion for inhalation, and tincture preparations for oral use.
region: Native range is Australia; the species is now cultivated in multiple regions globally.
summary: Eucalyptus globulus leaf is used in traditional herbal medicine for symptomatic relief of common-cold cough symptoms, with oral infusion and inhalation preparations described in EMA monographs.
